# c-mo3o0euk-054bbde3ae5f [live]
> Scope the MVP Patron Anima — what's in the first prototype, what's deferred, and what design questions do we need to answer before commissioning it?
Status: live
Created: 2026-04-18T01:35:14.732Z

## c-mo3o28hb-a5fa7b0a95f9 [concluded]
> What does Patron Anima output — challenge questions, veto votes, annotations on proposed decisions, or something else?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Patron Anima emits, per decision:
- **verdict**: confirm / override / fill-in (mandatory; no abstain, no surface-flip)
- **selection**: picked from analyst's offered options only (no custom — human patron keeps that escape hatch; anima is deliberately more constrained for measurable, type-safe output)
- **confidence**: low / med / high (mandatory from day one — don't treat as optional or it'll get skipped)

Applied to every decision in the plan (both surfaced and auto-decided). Output shape is identical to existing patron-feedback shape, so it plugs into decision-review engine without new plumbing.

**Override rate tracked as a first-class planner-quality instrument.** Override × confidence gives two quasi-orthogonal signals:
- high override + high confidence → planner is weak on taste-relevant decisions
- high override + low confidence → anima is flailing
- high confirm + low confidence → anima doesn't know patron well enough
- high confirm + high confidence → system is working

**Low-confidence handling default: don't raise to patron.** Flip to 'raise if confidence < X' only if metrics show problems.
Created: 2026-04-18T01:36:39.791Z
Resolved: 2026-04-18T01:52:09.724Z

### c-mo3qj676-ed75775ba337 [live]
> Design the BA/PO pre-plan stage — produce a clarified-feature-spec from the brief via extract, extrapolate, and interview; becomes the input contract for the reader-analyst phase
Status: live
Created: 2026-04-18T02:45:49.218Z

#### c-mo3qj9by-ed5ad14e41d6 [concluded]
> Define the shape of the clarified-feature-spec — required/optional sections, minimum content per section, field types, and validation rules for 'this spec is complete enough to hand to the analyst'
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Final shape of the clarified-feature-spec. Six sections total.

| Section | Tier | Structure |
|---|---|---|
| Summary | Required | Prose paragraph, ~500 chars, describes what the feature is and who it's for |
| Behaviors | Required (≥1) | List of {id: B1..., trigger, outcome, source} — uniform records regardless of flow type |
| Acceptance Criteria | Required (≥1) | List of {id: AC1..., statement, refs to behaviors} — each behavior must be covered by ≥1 AC |
| Out of Scope | Required-presence | List of explicit non-goals; can be 'nothing notable' but must be addressed |
| Edge Cases | Required-presence | **References-only** — list of {behavior-id, one-line rationale}; classification layer pointing into Behaviors |
| Open Questions | Required-presence | List of {question, context, confidence} or empty; forces BA self-check |

**Sections explicitly dropped from initial shape** (see sibling clicks for rationale):
- Dependencies (c-mo3r9a8s) — requires canon-research / feature catalog infrastructure we don't have
- Success Metrics (c-mo3r9auv) — aspirational; verification umbrella handles this downstream
- Use Cases (c-mo3rhkrh) — fabrication and scope-creep risk; gap filled by AC and Edge Cases

**Cross-cutting rules:**
- **Provenance tagging:** every item in Behaviors and optional-anchored positions must cite source (brief | extrapolated from [anchor] | interview Q[n]). Creates auditability and prevents fabrication.
- **User-observability test:** every outcome in Behaviors must be user-visible (BA domain). Implementation-visible-only changes belong to the analyst, not the BA.
- **Cross-section linking:** AC ids reference behavior ids; Edge Cases entries reference behavior ids. Stable identifiers enable machine-checkable coverage.

**Validation rule for 'complete enough to hand to analyst':**
- All required sections non-empty with real content (not placeholders)
- ≥1 behavior and ≥1 AC
- Every behavior covered by ≥1 AC
- Required-presence sections addressed (can be 'none' but must say so)

**Governing principle** (inherited from the broader BA/PO stage role split): BA resolves product-layer ambiguity; reader/analyst resolves implementation ambiguity. Sections that invite speculation beyond the BA's accessible sources (brief, referenced material, interview) were trimmed.
Created: 2026-04-18T02:45:53.278Z
Resolved: 2026-04-18T03:24:58.263Z

##### c-mo3r9a8s-1d9bb8e02477 [concluded]
> Dependencies section — why excluded from initial shape and when to revisit
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Excluded from initial feature-spec shape. The BA has no reliable source for product-level system knowledge beyond what's explicitly named in the brief. The three sources the BA can reach — brief text, referenced material (concluded clicks/docs the brief links), Patron Anima interview — don't include a queryable feature catalog. The anima is a judgment layer for proposed content, not a retrieval layer for existing content; asking it to enumerate related features invites fabrication from implicit training knowledge.

Revisit when canon-research (c-mo3d6pkh) or atlas (c-mo1mqhql) lands. At that point either (a) canon-research populates Dependencies before BA runs, or (b) BA queries canon-research during its pass. Until then, dependencies named in the brief are visible to the analyst directly; a dedicated section is redundant and risky.
Created: 2026-04-18T03:06:07.516Z
Resolved: 2026-04-18T03:06:14.800Z

##### c-mo3r9auv-7124ed1f0aa7 [concluded]
> Success Metrics section — why excluded from initial shape and when to revisit
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Excluded from initial feature-spec shape. Metrics are inherently aspirational/future-state — the BA has no way to validate them or tie them to real measurement infrastructure. Risk of aspirational fabrication is high: the BA can produce plausible-sounding metrics that nobody tracks, creating false signal for the analyst (implying success criteria beyond Acceptance Criteria) and inviting scope creep.

Revisit after the verification umbrella (c-mo3dj3u3) lands. Verification work will produce real measurement infrastructure and define what 'working in production' looks like structurally. Once that exists, Success Metrics as a feature-spec section becomes grounded in concrete instrumentation rather than speculation.
Created: 2026-04-18T03:06:08.311Z
Resolved: 2026-04-18T03:06:18.174Z

##### c-mo3rhkrh-d6ace5546b49 [concluded]
> Use Cases section — why excluded from initial shape
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Excluded from initial feature-spec shape. Use cases are narrative scenarios describing how behaviors compose into user flows, but in practice they are where BA fabrication is most tempting — every 'what if the user also wants X' is an invitation to invent scope the brief never committed to.

**Risks:**
- Fabricated scenarios that invent user goals not in the brief (high)
- Scope creep via narrative (medium-to-high) — a use case can imply behaviors that aren't in the Behaviors section
- Redundancy with behaviors + summary for simple features (low individual cost but clutter)

**The gap isn't unfilled without this section:**
- Implications like 'cancelled = completion for scheduling' can be captured as Acceptance Criteria ('AC: A cancelled blocker unblocks its dependents') or as Edge Cases
- Briefs often already contain narrative prose; the analyst can read the raw brief directly
- Atomic behaviors plus AC are sufficient for most features

**Precedent alignment:** consistent with dropping Success Metrics and Dependencies — each section that invites BA speculation beyond accessible sources gets trimmed. Simpler BA role, smaller surface for prompts to go off-script.

Revisit only if pattern emerges where analyst consistently misses compositions that atomic behaviors don't make obvious.
Created: 2026-04-18T03:12:34.397Z
Resolved: 2026-04-18T03:12:48.762Z

##### c-mo3rwsju-b7c6c08c3b93 [concluded]
> Edge Cases section design — references-only pattern pointing to behaviors with one-line rationale, required-presence
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Edge Cases section is kept as **required-presence** but contains **only references to behaviors** — not standalone content.

**Structure per entry:** behavior id + one-line rationale (format b).

Example:
```
Edge Cases:
- B3 — cancelled-blocker case (symmetric to completion)
- B5 — simultaneous release of shared blocker
- B7 — malformed blocked_by entry
```

**Rationale for references-only design:**
- Edge cases ARE behaviors with unusual triggers — the distinction is classification, not semantic. Keeping them in Behaviors (uniform records) avoids fuzzy boundaries between sections and eliminates the 'is this a behavior or edge case?' judgment call.
- Tagging each behavior record with a 'flow' field felt awkward; a dedicated classification section is cleaner.
- The rationale per reference makes the section self-contained for human readers without forcing cross-reference; it also documents *why* the BA classified it as edge, which is useful signal.

**Validation rules for edge-case behaviors:**
- Must pass the user-observability test (outcome is user-visible)
- Must be sourced from (1) brief explicit mention, (3) behavior-trigger symmetry extrapolation, or (4) interview confirmation. Not from vague universal-quantifier implications (source 2) or BA invention (source 5).

**Required-presence status** forces the BA to explicitly consider 'any edge cases?' rather than silently skip. Empty list is valid; omission is not.
Created: 2026-04-18T03:24:24.330Z
Resolved: 2026-04-18T03:24:37.599Z

###### c-mo3s410k-e9dd50be7a03 [live]
> Operational criteria for edge-case classification — what test does the BA apply to decide whether a behavior belongs in the Edge Cases section vs being presented as main flow? The structural design is settled (references-only), but 'edge vs main' is currently BA judgment with no codified criterion. Candidate tests: frequency-of-path (rare triggers = edge), casual-reader-expectation (would a reader expect this behavior to exist? if no → edge), symmetry-status (explicitly-named trigger = main, symmetrically-implied trigger = edge).
Status: live
Created: 2026-04-18T03:30:01.892Z

Status: live

> BA specialization — is there one generic BA role applied to every brief, or a family of specialized BAs (BA-for-new-feature, BA-for-refactor, BA-for-bugfix, etc.)? Mirror of the Patron Anima singleton-vs-family question (c-mo3o2b49) but for the interviewer role.
Status: live
Created: 2026-04-18T03:28:42.392Z

#### c-mo3qjbmw-1b90e34a9174 [live]
> Design the BA process — decision criteria and ordering for when to extract (from the brief), extrapolate (from context), or interview (ask the anima); what triggers each, and what's the handoff between them?
Status: live
Created: 2026-04-18T02:45:56.264Z

Status: live
Created: 2026-04-18T02:45:57.848Z

#### c-mo3qsanr-25fdfddb04be [live]
> Design reader-analyst input contract — both the raw brief and clarified-feature-spec live on the PlanDoc, but does the analyst receive the whole PlanDoc or just the BA-produced feature-spec?
Status: live
Created: 2026-04-18T02:52:54.903Z

> Validation of BA output — before reader-analyst runs, what verifies the clarified-feature-spec is structurally and semantically complete? MVP is 'best effort' by analyst. Future options: self-check (required fields present), anima-verified (Patron Anima confirms), patron-gated.
Status: parked

> BA fallback behavior when the brief is too thin for a valid feature-spec even with full interviewing — MVP is 'best effort' with whatever was produced. Future options: escalate to patron, mark pending, block pipeline.

- Must pass the user-observability test (outcome is user-visible)
- Must be sourced from (1) brief explicit mention, (3) behavior-trigger symmetry extrapolation, or (4) interview confirmation. Not from vague universal-quantifier implications (source 2) or BA invention (source 5).

**Required-presence status** forces the BA to explicitly consider 'any edge cases?' rather than silently skip. Empty list is valid; omission is not.

content.text:
Good — full context loaded. This is a foundational question for the MVP Patron Anima: without a taste corpus, the anima has nothing distinctive to stand on. Let me lay out what I've worked through.

## The shape of the question

Three sub-questions bundled together:

1. **What sources carry taste signal?** (inventory)
2. **What does the anima actually see at inference time?** (selection — because the full inventory won't fit)
3. **How does the corpus stay current as taste drifts?** (freshness)

They're coupled: inventory determines ceiling, selection determines prompt economics, freshness determines whether the anima tracks reality or a fossil. Worth analyzing all three before picking the MVP cut.

## Inventory — ranked by taste-signal density

| Source | Signal | Volume | Cost to consume |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Concluded clicks** (conclusions) | **Highest.** Explicit decisions with stated trade-off reasoning. | Growing, hundreds now. | Medium — conclusions are dense. |
| **Patron feedback on plans** (existing decision-review output) | Highest-direct — already shaped like Patron Anima's output. | Small but growing with every planner run. | Low. |
| **Commission review comments** (when Sean annotates output) | High — raw taste on artifacts. | Sparse today; instrumented via `reviewed_at`. | Low. |
| **Philosophy / guild-metaphor docs** | High. Upstream principles shaping all decisions. | Small, stable. | Low. |
| **CLAUDE.md / coco.md / agent files** | High for *process*-taste (how to work) — lower for decision-taste. | Small, edited frequently. | Low. |
| **Design session transcripts** | Very high density but extremely noisy — contains rejected ideas, iterations, false starts. | Large and growing. | High. |
| **Coco chat transcripts** | Similar to design sessions — raw, sprawling. | Large and growing. | High. |
| **Ethnographer interviews & session-notes** | Pre-curated taste signal. | Small, hand-maintained. | Low. |
| **Commission log** (complexity ratings, verdicts, retries) | Weak, indirect — behavioral. | Growing. | Low. |
| **Coco log** (commissionable=true/false) | Weak, indirect — what Sean considers autonomous-worthy. | Growing. | Low. |
| **Click tree shape** (live/parked/dropped patterns) | Weak, indirect — what he cares about vs shelves. | Derivable. | Low. |
| **Git history + commit messages** | Medium — what actually shipped in his voice. | Large. | High. |
| **Briefs drafted vs posted** (scratch trail) | Medium — acceptance/rejection of proposals. | Small. | Medium. |

The top four rows are where the corpus starts. Everything below is either pre-digested already (ethnography) or better left as on-demand retrieval.

## Selection — what the anima actually sees

The prompt-budget reality rules out "show the anima everything." Four architectures:

1. **Full corpus in prompt** — infeasible. Rule out.
2. **Curated digest only** — a compact "current state of Sean's taste" document always in context. Fast, but limited to what the digest captures.
3. **Pre-selected retrieval** — an upstream agent (reader-analyst? the BA?) retrieves K relevant items per decision and injects them. Anima doesn't manage retrieval.
4. **Digest + tool-driven retrieval** — anima always has the digest, plus can query concluded-clicks/docs for specifics when the digest is too abstract for the decision at hand.

**My recommendation: 3 for MVP, 4 as the evolutionary target.**

Rationale: option 3 is simpler and tests the primary thesis (does injected Sean-taste-context change verdict quality?) without also testing "can the anima be a good retrieval user?" That's two unknowns; MVP should isolate one. Once 3 works, upgrade the anima to 4 when the digest starts missing decisions it should be catching.

## Freshness — three layers, two cadences

The corpus shouldn't be a single file that goes stale. Propose three layers:

- **Raw layer** — append-only archive of everything in the inventory table. No curation, no deletion. Ground truth.
- **Index layer** — machine-maintained metadata over the raw layer: per-item taste-tags, timestamps, supersession edges (when a new click explicitly overrides an older one), confidence. Updated automatically on every new concluded click, review, or brief. This is what retrieval queries against.
- **Digest layer** — a living human-approved "current taste" summary. Short enough to fit in every prompt. Drafted by Coco, approved by Sean, committed to the sanctum.

**Cadences:**
- **Automatic** — index updates on every new concluded click, review, or commit.
- **Periodic** — digest regenerated at session wrap-up (natural checkpoint) when Coco notices new material or contradictions. Sean approves diff.

**Contradiction handling as a first-class feature:** when the index detects the corpus contains contradictory signals on the same axis (e.g., an old click says X, a new click says not-X, no supersession link between them), the anima gets flagged context: "patron has said both X and ¬X, unresolved." The anima should lower confidence or refuse verdict. This turns taste-drift from a silent corruption into a visible event.

## A feedback loop worth naming

The **override rate instrument** (already settled in the sibling click) is not just a quality diagnostic — it's a corpus-updating signal. Every patron override = the corpus was wrong about Sean's taste on that decision. Which means:

- High-override axes → digest refresh candidates
- Persistent override on a specific principle → the digest should promote that principle from absent to explicit

This makes the Patron Anima self-improving without requiring anyone to manually curate. Worth building in from day one even if the refresh step is manual.

## Proposed MVP cut

For the smallest useful version:

- **Inventory:** concluded clicks (conclusions only) + CLAUDE files + philosophy/guild-metaphor docs. That's it. Skip transcripts, skip git history, skip behavioral signals for v1.
- **Selection:** pre-selected retrieval (arch 3). Reader-analyst or planner runs a simple keyword/semantic match against concluded clicks per decision, pulls top-K, injects. No tools for the anima yet.
- **Freshness:** no index, no digest. Just the live click tree + current-state docs. This makes the v1 corpus trivially fresh because it *is* the current state. The digest-and-index layers get built when retrieval is too slow or too noisy to sustain.

That defers the digest question entirely to v2 — which I think is right, because the digest's format and maintenance cadence is where a lot of hidden design lives, and we'll learn what to put in it by watching v1's retrieval fail.

## Open questions I'd like to resolve with you

1. **Retrieval ownership** — does the reader-analyst do the retrieval pre-anima, or does the planner do it? This bleeds into c-mo3o292o (Patron Anima pipeline position).
2. **Transcript inclusion** — I'm skeptical transcripts are worth the complexity for MVP; they're the highest-signal unstructured source but also where fabrication is easiest. Agree to defer?
3. **Digest format** — when we eventually need one, should it look more like a "principles list" (anti-fabrication, prefer-small-artifacts, etc.) or more like a structured "on decision type D, Sean prefers X axis"? Both are defensible; I lean principles-first because it's how Sean actually talks.

Where do you want to push first?

content: We're scoping the Patron Anima taste corpus — what sources carry Sean's taste, how the anima sees it, how it stays fresh. I've laid out inventory, selection, and freshness analysis plus a proposed MVP cut; next action is your call on where to push first. (disable recaps in /config)

You're right — I missed them, and they're actually a better corpus candidate than transcripts for almost every use case. Let me correct the analysis.

## Where session notes fit

Reading the 2026-04-07 note confirms the hunch: "Notable moments" sections capture direct Sean quotes *with Coco's framing of why the pushback mattered* — which is exactly the kind of pre-distilled taste signal we want. "Decisions made" and "Next steps" give decision-bounded structure. Coco-authored, but with Sean's voice quoted verbatim where it counted.

Updated inventory (inserting a new row, re-ranking):

| Source | Signal | Volume | Cost to consume |
|---|---|---|---|
| Concluded clicks (conclusions) | **Highest — direct.** Sean approved the text. | Hundreds, growing. | Medium. |
| Patron feedback on plans | Highest — directly shaped like anima output. | Sparse now. | Low. |
| Commission review comments | High — raw taste on artifacts. | Sparse. | Low. |
| **Session notes (`session-notes/reviewed/`)** | **High — Coco-distilled, session-bounded, with Sean quotes in "Notable moments".** | Medium, one per session. | **Low.** |
| **Session notes (`session-notes/new/`)** | **High but provisional — not yet reviewed.** | Small rolling buffer. | **Low.** |
| Philosophy / guild-metaphor docs | High. Upstream principles. | Small, stable. | Low. |
| CLAUDE.md / agent files | High for process-taste. | Small. | Low. |
| Design session transcripts | Very high density, very noisy. | Large. | High. |
| Coco chat transcripts | Same. | Large. | High. |
| Ethnographer interviews | Pre-curated. | Small. | Low. |
| Commission log / Coco log | Weak behavioral signal. | Growing. | Low. |
| Click tree shape | Weak, indirect. | Derivable. | Low. |
| Git history | Medium. | Large. | High. |
| Briefs drafted vs posted | Medium. | Small. | Medium. |

## Two things this changes

**1. Session notes displace transcripts for MVP.** I was going to defer transcripts entirely because they're high-volume and noisy. Session notes give us ~90% of the same signal at ~5% of the consumption cost — they *are* the distilled transcript. New MVP inventory:

- Concluded clicks (conclusions only)
- **Session notes — `reviewed/` directory, all of them**
- Philosophy / guild-metaphor / CLAUDE files

That's still small enough to work with but captures the in-session push-backs transcripts would have given us.

**2. The new/ → reviewed/ pipeline is a freshness mechanism we already have.** The ethnographer workflow already distinguishes provisional (new/) from validated (reviewed/) notes. Patron Anima can consume from `reviewed/` by default and optionally pull from `new/` with a provisionality flag. No new pipeline needed.

## One honest caveat worth naming

Concluded-click conclusions are taste signal Sean explicitly approved (conclusion text committed with his review). Session notes are *Coco's interpretation of what mattered to Sean* — faithful in practice but one layer of indirection. For Patron Anima corpus weighting, I'd treat them as:

- **Concluded clicks:** direct, load-bearing
- **Session notes (reviewed):** supporting, frame the "why" around decisions
- **Session notes (new):** provisional, discount until reviewed

Practical consequence: if Patron Anima ever contradicts itself across sources, concluded clicks win over session notes win over new session notes. A tidy hierarchy that also matches how much human validation each tier has received.

Want me to keep going on the other open questions (retrieval ownership, digest format), or dig into anything else here?

content.text:
This is a much cleaner architecture than what I was sketching, and it collapses a lot of complexity. Let me work through what it changes.

## The clean reframe

The question is no longer "how do we build a taste corpus pipeline" — it's "how do we *author a role file* that represents Sean's taste." Runtime mechanics disappear:

- **Retrieval goes away.** Role instructions = system prompt = static per run. No query-time assembly, no K-most-relevant logic, no retrieval-ownership question.
- **Freshness becomes docs-maintenance.** "When do we refresh the role file?" is a human/Coco cadence question, not a pipeline-engineering question.
- **The digest IS the role file.** The raw/index/digest three-layer thing I proposed collapses to one layer. Inventory sources (clicks, session notes, philosophy, CLAUDE files) are now *inputs to authoring*, not *runtime sources*.
- **Volume becomes a hard ceiling.** System prompt is finite context. We *can't* stuff hundreds of click conclusions in. Curation isn't optional — it's the whole game. The question I was treating as v2 (digest format) is now v1's only real design question.

This also makes the thing **guild-portable by construction**. Another patron instantiating their own guild writes their own role file. The framework provides the slot (Astrolabe config → role name lookup), the guild fills it. No sanctum-specific leakage into the framework.

## The default-patron question

Two options you named: skip if unconfigured, or ship a framework-default based on local clicks.

**I'd strongly recommend skip-if-unconfigured.** Reasons:

- A "default patron" is a contradiction — every patron has different taste by definition. A framework default represents *no patron's* taste, which is incoherent rather than neutral.
- A clicks-only default *would* be guild-specific (clicks live in the guild), but it'd be a very thin anima — no sanctum docs, no session notes. The effort of authoring a real role file is 80% of the value; a clicks-only default delivers maybe 20%.
- Running a quality-degraded anima as default risks poisoning trust in the mechanism. Better to have the planner pipeline degrade gracefully to its pre-patron-anima shape and let patrons opt in when they're ready to do the authoring work.

The framework hands you the hook. You have to fill it to use it.

## What this means for siblings in the scope-MVP tree

Worth flagging because several sibling clicks get reshaped:

- **c-mo3o2b49 (one anima or family)** — becomes trivially representable. Family = multiple role files (patron-on-architecture, patron-on-ergonomics). The question reduces to "do we author one file or several?" Much cheaper to answer, and cheaper to change later.
- **c-mo3o2bqi (self-uncertainty handling)** — partially reduces to prompt engineering on the role file (e.g., role instructions say "when the corpus doesn't clearly indicate patron preference, lower confidence and flag"). Still substantive but scoped.
- **c-mo3o2cm3 (MVP cut)** — the MVP cut just became mostly "author the first role file." Dramatically simpler than what I was heading toward.

## What remains the real question

How the role file is structured. Two shapes on the table from earlier:

- **Principles-list** — "prefer small, well-grounded artifacts over aspirationally-complete ones; be wary of fabrication when extrapolating beyond stated sources; prefer explicit contracts over implicit shared state" — the stuff Sean actually says when articulating taste.
- **Structured-by-decision-type** — "on architecture decisions, Sean weights X; on ergonomics, Y; on API shape, Z."

My read: principles-list for v1, because it matches how Sean talks and how the high-signal sources (concluded clicks, session-note "Notable moments") actually read. The decision-type axis can be layered on later if we find the principles list isn't discriminating enough between decision domains.

## Proposed conclusion shape for this click

If you're aligned, I'd land this click as something like:

> Corpus is not a runtime retrieval pipeline — it's the *input set* to authoring a guild-specific **patron role file** installed via Astrolabe config. Role file = system prompt for the anima, prose format. Framework provides the configuration slot and skips patron steps when unconfigured (no default). Sanctum-side authoring draws from concluded clicks, session notes, philosophy/guild-metaphor docs, and CLAUDE files; transcripts deferred as too high-cost for the marginal signal over session notes. Freshness is docs-maintenance cadence, not runtime staleness management; override-rate instrument is the primary signal for when refresh is needed. File structure: principles-list for v1. Curation of volume is the central authoring challenge.

Does that capture it, or are there pieces you'd want represented differently before I conclude?

This opens some questions on how we can dynamically shape rigs and prompts and such in other areas, but i think the principle is solid enough for the reasons you stated that we should land onit. I would like to just call out a future state, where role instructions can be produced by a function() instead of just a pointer to an md file. That would unlock a whole world of possibility for anima construction. otherwise, i'd say you can conclude with your propsoed languagge

---
description: Work with clicks — Coco's session-continuity mechanism. Invoke when opening, updating, resuming, or concluding a line of inquiry that should outlive the current session.
---

# Clicks — Session-Continuity Workflow

> NOTE: The environment should have a properly defined `GUILD_ROOT` allowing the `nsg` command to be used without `--guild-root`. If this is not the case, the guild root (at `/workspace/vibers`) can be appended to commands as needed.

A **click** is an atomic decision-node managed by the Ratchet apparatus. Each click captures one question or inquiry; when resolved, it records the conclusion. Clicks are organized in a tree — children are sub-questions of their parent. Together, the click tree forms a structured record of the reasoning and decisions that guide the guild's work.

Clicks are Coco's primary mechanism for session continuity, replacing the earlier quest writ type. Key differences from quests:

- **Goal is immutable.** Set at creation, never changes. If the framing is wrong, drop and create a new one.
- **Conclusion is write-once.** Set by `conclude` or `drop`, then frozen.
- **No body files.** Click goals are short (one sentence). Long-form exploration lives in session transcripts, joinable via session ID.
- **The tree is the product.** Value lives in structure (hierarchy, decomposition), not prose. Children are the todo list.
- **Four statuses:** `live | parked | concluded | dropped`. Simple, designed for inquiry.

## When to open a click

Open a click when a conversation or line of thought has enough substance to outlive the current session. Heuristics:

- Sean is exploring a design question across multiple turns
- You've tabled something with "let's come back to this"
- A decision is deferred pending more thought
- A line of inquiry has its own arc — opening question, accumulating sub-questions, eventual conclusion

Don't open a click for every exchange. One-off questions, quick clarifications, and routine operational work don't need one.

## Creating a click

    nsg click create --goal "How should we handle X?" [--parent-id <id>]

- The `--goal` is the question or inquiry — frame it as a question when possible.
- Use `--parent-id` to nest under an existing click (sub-question decomposition).
- The click starts in `live` status.

### Vocabulary discovery habit

When opening any new click, scan `docs/future/guild-vocabulary.md` for related terms and consider creating cross-links. The vocabulary tome holds latent metaphor concepts that imply future features; the only mechanism that surfaces them at the right moment is this manual habit.

## Viewing clicks

Two commands do the orientation work. Reach for them in this order:

**1. Tree view** — board-level scan, the first thing you run at startup:

    nsg click tree [--root-id <id>] [--status live] [--depth N]

Renders the click forest with box-drawing connectors and status indicators:
```
● How should the quest system evolve?                    [live]
  ├── ○ Is the friction removable or structural?         [concluded]
  ├── ● What should we call these things?                [live]
  └── ◇ Do quests need an event-log layer?               [parked]
```
Status indicators: `●` live, `◇` parked, `○` concluded, `✕` dropped.

**2. Extract a subtree** — the primary narrative-loading command, used whenever the conversation turns toward a specific area:

    nsg click extract --id <id> [--format md|json]

One call loads the whole subtree — goals, statuses, and conclusions for any concluded/dropped clicks — as a structured document. This is how you orient on any line of inquiry.

**Don't do this:** don't walk a subtree by calling `show` on each child to reconstruct the narrative. That's what `extract` is for. If you find yourself running `show` three or more times in a row to understand the shape of an area, stop and run `extract` on the common ancestor instead.

**Show a single click** — use only for single-click inspection (checking one click's links, parent, status, or conclusion):

    nsg click show <id>

**List with filters:**

    nsg click list [--status live] [--status parked] [--limit 50]

## Status transitions

```
live ──> parked       (park — deliberately dormant, pick up later)
parked ──> live       (resume — back to active exploration)
live ──> concluded    (conclude — decision reached, conclusion required)
live ──> dropped      (drop — abandoned, reason required)
parked ──> concluded  (conclude directly from parked)
parked ──> dropped    (drop directly from parked)
```

Terminal states (concluded, dropped) are immutable — no further changes allowed.

**Park** a click (live -> parked):

    nsg click park --id <id>

**Resume** a parked click (parked -> live):

    nsg click resume --id <id>

**Conclude** a click with a decision:

    nsg click conclude --id <id> --conclusion "We decided to..."

**Drop** a click without a decision:

    nsg click drop --id <id> --conclusion "Moot because..."

### Conclusions are tight

A conclusion is a pointer-note for future-me, not an essay for posterity. Target shape: **decision + one-line reasoning + (optional) one-clause naming the rejected alternatives.** A small paragraph, not a screenful.

- **No markdown structure.** No headers, no tables, no "Recommendation:" sections. If a conclusion needs structure, it's too long.
- **Don't re-argue rejected options.** Name them in a clause ("rejected `spider.sequence` for directional ambiguity"); don't rebuild the tradeoff table.
- **Exploration stays in the session transcript.** The analytical work — enumerated cases, stress tests, option matrices — belongs in chat, joinable via session ID. Only the distilled outcome lands in the conclusion.

Verbose conclusions are the main context cost of clicks. Keep them short and the tree can grow freely.

## Sub-questions (children)

When a click spawns distinct sub-inquiries, create child clicks:

    nsg click create --goal "Sub-question A?" --parent-id <parent-id>

Children are the decomposition mechanism. Resolving a parent typically means resolving or dropping its children. But there's no automatic cascading — a parent can be concluded while children are still live (e.g., "we answered the main question; remaining sub-questions are moot").

## Links

Typed links connect clicks to each other or to writs (cross-substrate):

    nsg click link --source-id <click-id> --target-id <target-id> --link-type <type>

Link types:
- `related` — lateral cross-reference
- `commissioned` — this click's conclusion produced a commission (target is a writ ID)
- `supersedes` — this click replaces the target
- `depends-on` — this click can't be concluded until the target is resolved

**Commissioned links are automatic.** When a click's conclusion spawns a writ (brief posted, commission dispatched), create the `commissioned` link from the click to the writ id without asking. This preserves the click-to-artifact trail in the tree and is cheap to maintain. If multiple clicks contribute to one commission, link the narrowest conclusion-bearing click; add additional links from contributing parents only if the trail would be lossy otherwise.

Remove a link:

    nsg click unlink --source-id <id> --target-id <id> --link-type <type>

## Reparenting

Move a click to a new parent or to root:

    nsg click reparent --id <id> --parent-id <new-parent-id>
    nsg click reparent --id <id>                               # move to root

Circular parentage is detected and rejected.

## Short ID support

All ID parameters accept short prefixes (e.g., `c-mo1ee` instead of the full ID). The Ratchet resolves prefixes and errors on ambiguity.

## Clicks and session continuity

Clicks provide continuity across sessions. The flow is:

- **Startup** — scan the click tree with `nsg click tree --status live --status parked` to see what's in flight. When the conversation turns toward a specific area, use `nsg click extract --id <id>` to load the full subtree as narrative context. Reserve `nsg click show <id>` for single-click inspection. Don't eagerly read every click.
- **During the session** — open new clicks for substantial inquiries; create child clicks for sub-questions. Conclude or drop clicks as decisions are reached.
- **Wrap-up** — park any live clicks that won't be continued immediately. Conclude clicks where decisions were reached this session. The click tree itself is the session's durable record of what was explored and decided.

Because click goals are immutable and conclusions are write-once, there's no body to maintain or keep in sync. The session transcript (joinable via session ID) holds the full exploration context. Clicks are just the structural skeleton — questions asked and answers given.

content.text:
Moving to **c-mo3o2b49 — one anima or a family**. This is the question I flagged earlier as partially collapsed by the role-based architecture; let me do the full analysis.

## Reframe given the role-based architecture

"Family" just means multiple role files routed per decision. Nothing exotic — same machinery, N instantiations. So the question isn't about feasibility, it's about **cost vs benefit of splitting**.

## The real trade-off

**One patron:**
- Single role file to maintain; one authoring cadence
- Holistic — can reason across axes (a decision that's simultaneously architecture + ergonomics)
- Risk: grab-bag file; taste nuances from different domains get averaged into generic principles

**Family (patron-on-architecture, patron-on-ergonomics, etc.):**
- Each role tighter and more opinionated within its domain
- Per-domain override rates are easier to diagnose
- Authoring burden multiplies (N files to keep current)
- **Routing problem** — something has to decide which patron sees which decision. Options all cost something:
  - Static mapping from decision-type → patron: requires analyst to emit typed domain tags (new contract)
  - Fan-out to all patrons per decision: wasteful, most return "not my domain"
  - Opt-in: every patron reads every decision, even more wasteful
- Cross-domain decisions fall through cracks or require arbitration

## The key question I had to chew on

**Is Sean's taste-across-domains actually different, or is it the same underlying principles applied to different surfaces?**

I think it's mostly the latter. The principles that keep showing up — "prefer small, well-grounded artifacts," "beware fabrication from structural hints," "prefer explicit contracts over implicit shared state" — apply to architecture, API shape, and brief content pretty uniformly. The principles-list format we picked for v1 actively *emphasizes* this; principles are domain-transferable by nature.

Where does domain-specific texture show up? Things like TypeScript idiom preferences, CLI ergonomics, commit-message style — but those already live in CLAUDE files and coding-conventions docs and feed implementers directly. They're not plan-decision-anima input. Different channel, already handled.

So the underlying taste looks unified. The family architecture would be mostly overhead.

## What we'd lose by staying single

Main diagnostic loss: **we can't tell which domain of taste is drifting when override rate climbs.** One patron = one override rate signal.

But that's fixable without splitting the patron. **If decisions carry a domain tag (from the analyst), the same override-rate instrument can be sliced by domain** — same diagnostic signal, no multiplied authoring burden. Domain is a property of the decision, not of the verdict, so tagging lives upstream of the anima.

## Recommendation — single patron for MVP, with a forcing-function trigger for the split

One role file. Principles-list format (already settled). Decisions optionally carry a domain tag for diagnostic slicing.

Revisit the family question if any of these emerge:
- Consistent per-domain overrides showing the generalist can't capture a specific domain's taste in unified principles
- Role file growing beyond what fits in context cleanly (splitting becomes forced)
- Natural sectioning inside the single file ("take-on-architecture" / "take-on-naming" sections) — at which point splitting is trivial

This is the "wait for the forcing function" approach. Don't build family-of-patrons speculatively; let it evolve if and when the single patron starts showing domain-specific stress.

## One small adjacent question surfaced

**Should decisions carry a domain tag?** Lightweight schema addition on the analyst side, enables per-domain override diagnostics, doesn't force the family split. Worth capturing as a follow-up under the planner pipeline work (not patron MVP scope). I can spawn that as a top-level or under the evidence-based decisional machinery tree if you want.

## Proposed conclusion shape

> Single patron role for MVP. Family architecture is representationally trivial (multiple role files + routing) but mostly overhead: Sean's taste across domains is largely unified principles applied to different surfaces, and per-domain diagnostic slicing can be achieved by tagging decisions (analyst-side) rather than splitting the patron. Forcing functions for eventual split: persistent per-domain overrides the generalist can't capture, role file outgrowing context, or natural sectioning inside the single file. Surface-specific texture (TS idioms, CLI ergonomics) lives in CLAUDE/convention docs and feeds implementers directly, not a decision anima. Adjacent follow-up worth tracking: should decisions carry a domain tag for diagnostic slicing.

Aligned, or is there a pull toward the family side I'm underweighting?

message.content: is tehre something we could add to your tool/instructions so that clicking that would have bveen obvious and automatic without prompting?

content.text:
Good meta-question. Let me diagnose honestly, then propose something concrete.

## What went wrong

I had everything I needed to do it automatically. The inventory table enumerated 14 sources with per-item signal/volume/cost analysis — each row was effectively a latent click with an implicit include/exclude conclusion. I collapsed that enumeration into a summary in the parent's conclusion instead of lifting it into structure.

Why? Two causes, in order of severity:

1. **I was self-censoring on tree-clutter grounds.** 14 clicks felt like a lot, so I subconsciously avoided the move. This is exactly what the "capture liberally — the tree is cheap" note in coco.md warns against, and I walked right past it. The abstract principle didn't fire when the specific trigger presented itself.

2. **No concrete recognition pattern.** The clicks skill's "Sub-questions (children)" section is pretty abstract — "when a click spawns distinct sub-inquiries, create child clicks." It doesn't name the *shape* I was staring at (an enumerated-items analysis with per-item decisions hiding in a summary).

## Proposed addition to the clicks skill

Adding a named pattern with a concrete trigger that would have fired on exactly this situation:

```
### Enumerated decisions → enumerated children

When the analysis inside a click naturally enumerates N items
with per-item evaluations (N sources → include/exclude each,
N options → pick-one across them, N candidates → accept/reject each),
those evaluations are latent sub-clicks. Lift them into children
rather than collapsing them into the parent's conclusion.

**Recognition trigger:** if you catch yourself writing a conclusion
that contains a per-item list with individual rationale ("source A:
yes because X; source B: no because Y; ..."), stop — that list is
telling you the decisions want their own clicks. The parent's
conclusion should name the *principle*; each child's conclusion
carries one item's rationale.

This aligns with conclusion-discipline (keeps each conclusion tight,
no inline tables) and with capture-liberally (the tree is cheap;
per-item decisions are durable structural references worth having).
```

The recognition trigger is the key part — it names the specific editor-level symptom ("I'm writing a bulleted per-item list inside a conclusion") that indicates the tree should be deeper. That's the hook that fires *while I'm drafting*, before I commit the wrong shape.

This goes in the Sub-questions (children) section of `.claude/skills/clicks/SKILL.md`. I don't think coco.md needs a change — the "capture liberally" principle is already there at the right level; what was missing was the concrete trigger at the clicks-skill level.

Want me to make that edit?

---
description: Work with clicks — Coco's session-continuity mechanism. Invoke when opening, updating, resuming, or concluding a line of inquiry that should outlive the current session.
---

# Clicks — Session-Continuity Workflow

> NOTE: The environment should have a properly defined `GUILD_ROOT` allowing the `nsg` command to be used without `--guild-root`. If this is not the case, the guild root (at `/workspace/vibers`) can be appended to commands as needed.

A **click** is an atomic decision-node managed by the Ratchet apparatus. Each click captures one question or inquiry; when resolved, it records the conclusion. Clicks are organized in a tree — children are sub-questions of their parent. Together, the click tree forms a structured record of the reasoning and decisions that guide the guild's work.

Clicks are Coco's primary mechanism for session continuity, replacing the earlier quest writ type. Key differences from quests:

- **Goal is immutable.** Set at creation, never changes. If the framing is wrong, drop and create a new one.
- **Conclusion is write-once.** Set by `conclude` or `drop`, then frozen.
- **No body files.** Click goals are short (one sentence). Long-form exploration lives in session transcripts, joinable via session ID.
- **The tree is the product.** Value lives in structure (hierarchy, decomposition), not prose. Children are the todo list.
- **Four statuses:** `live | parked | concluded | dropped`. Simple, designed for inquiry.

## When to open a click

Open a click when a conversation or line of thought has enough substance to outlive the current session. Heuristics:

- Sean is exploring a design question across multiple turns
- You've tabled something with "let's come back to this"
- A decision is deferred pending more thought
- A line of inquiry has its own arc — opening question, accumulating sub-questions, eventual conclusion

Don't open a click for every exchange. One-off questions, quick clarifications, and routine operational work don't need one.

## Creating a click

    nsg click create --goal "How should we handle X?" [--parent-id <id>]

- The `--goal` is the question or inquiry — frame it as a question when possible.
- Use `--parent-id` to nest under an existing click (sub-question decomposition).
- The click starts in `live` status.

### Vocabulary discovery habit

When opening any new click, scan `docs/future/guild-vocabulary.md` for related terms and consider creating cross-links. The vocabulary tome holds latent metaphor concepts that imply future features; the only mechanism that surfaces them at the right moment is this manual habit.

## Viewing clicks

Two commands do the orientation work. Reach for them in this order:

**1. Tree view** — board-level scan, the first thing you run at startup:

    nsg click tree [--root-id <id>] [--status live] [--depth N]

Renders the click forest with box-drawing connectors and status indicators:
```
● How should the quest system evolve?                    [live]
  ├── ○ Is the friction removable or structural?         [concluded]
  ├── ● What should we call these things?                [live]
  └── ◇ Do quests need an event-log layer?               [parked]
```
Status indicators: `●` live, `◇` parked, `○` concluded, `✕` dropped.

**2. Extract a subtree** — the primary narrative-loading command, used whenever the conversation turns toward a specific area:

    nsg click extract --id <id> [--format md|json]

One call loads the whole subtree — goals, statuses, and conclusions for any concluded/dropped clicks — as a structured document. This is how you orient on any line of inquiry.

**Don't do this:** don't walk a subtree by calling `show` on each child to reconstruct the narrative. That's what `extract` is for. If you find yourself running `show` three or more times in a row to understand the shape of an area, stop and run `extract` on the common ancestor instead.

**Show a single click** — use only for single-click inspection (checking one click's links, parent, status, or conclusion):

    nsg click show <id>

**List with filters:**

    nsg click list [--status live] [--status parked] [--limit 50]

## Status transitions

```
live ──> parked       (park — deliberately dormant, pick up later)
parked ──> live       (resume — back to active exploration)
live ──> concluded    (conclude — decision reached, conclusion required)
live ──> dropped      (drop — abandoned, reason required)
parked ──> concluded  (conclude directly from parked)
parked ──> dropped    (drop directly from parked)
```

Terminal states (concluded, dropped) are immutable — no further changes allowed.

**Park** a click (live -> parked):

    nsg click park --id <id>

**Resume** a parked click (parked -> live):

    nsg click resume --id <id>

**Conclude** a click with a decision:

    nsg click conclude --id <id> --conclusion "We decided to..."

**Drop** a click without a decision:

    nsg click drop --id <id> --conclusion "Moot because..."

### Conclusions are tight

A conclusion is a pointer-note for future-me, not an essay for posterity. Target shape: **decision + one-line reasoning + (optional) one-clause naming the rejected alternatives.** A small paragraph, not a screenful.

- **No markdown structure.** No headers, no tables, no "Recommendation:" sections. If a conclusion needs structure, it's too long.
- **Don't re-argue rejected options.** Name them in a clause ("rejected `spider.sequence` for directional ambiguity"); don't rebuild the tradeoff table.
- **Exploration stays in the session transcript.** The analytical work — enumerated cases, stress tests, option matrices — belongs in chat, joinable via session ID. Only the distilled outcome lands in the conclusion.

Verbose conclusions are the main context cost of clicks. Keep them short and the tree can grow freely.

## Sub-questions (children)

When a click spawns distinct sub-inquiries, create child clicks:

    nsg click create --goal "Sub-question A?" --parent-id <parent-id>

Children are the decomposition mechanism. Resolving a parent typically means resolving or dropping its children. But there's no automatic cascading — a parent can be concluded while children are still live (e.g., "we answered the main question; remaining sub-questions are moot").

## Links

Typed links connect clicks to each other or to writs (cross-substrate):

    nsg click link --source-id <click-id> --target-id <target-id> --link-type <type>

Link types:
- `related` — lateral cross-reference
- `commissioned` — this click's conclusion produced a commission (target is a writ ID)
- `supersedes` — this click replaces the target
- `depends-on` — this click can't be concluded until the target is resolved

**Commissioned links are automatic.** When a click's conclusion spawns a writ (brief posted, commission dispatched), create the `commissioned` link from the click to the writ id without asking. This preserves the click-to-artifact trail in the tree and is cheap to maintain. If multiple clicks contribute to one commission, link the narrowest conclusion-bearing click; add additional links from contributing parents only if the trail would be lossy otherwise.

Remove a link:

    nsg click unlink --source-id <id> --target-id <id> --link-type <type>

## Reparenting

Move a click to a new parent or to root:

    nsg click reparent --id <id> --parent-id <new-parent-id>
    nsg click reparent --id <id>                               # move to root

Circular parentage is detected and rejected.

## Short ID support

All ID parameters accept short prefixes (e.g., `c-mo1ee` instead of the full ID). The Ratchet resolves prefixes and errors on ambiguity.

## Clicks and session continuity

Clicks provide continuity across sessions. The flow is:

- **Startup** — scan the click tree with `nsg click tree --status live --status parked` to see what's in flight. When the conversation turns toward a specific area, use `nsg click extract --id <id>` to load the full subtree as narrative context. Reserve `nsg click show <id>` for single-click inspection. Don't eagerly read every click.
- **During the session** — open new clicks for substantial inquiries; create child clicks for sub-questions. Conclude or drop clicks as decisions are reached.
- **Wrap-up** — park any live clicks that won't be continued immediately. Conclude clicks where decisions were reached this session. The click tree itself is the session's durable record of what was explored and decided.

Because click goals are immutable and conclusions are write-once, there's no body to maintain or keep in sync. The session transcript (joinable via session ID) holds the full exploration context. Clicks are just the structural skeleton — questions asked and answers given.
